query: current guidelines for managing magnesium sulfate toxicity including absence of deep tendon reflexes

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Magnesium Sulfate for Neuroprotection
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium sulfate is contraindicated in patients with myasthenia gravis and should be used with caution in patients with neuromuscular disease and heart block. This is excreted via the kidneys and thus requires renal dosing to prevent accumulation and magnesium toxicity in patients with renal dysfunction. All patients on magnesium sulfate should be carefully monitored for signs and symptoms of magnesium toxicity with regular assessments of their vital signs, urine output, deep tendon reflexes, and cardiopulmonary exams. With appropriate monitoring and care in high-resource settings, the 2024 Cochrane review found that magnesium sulfate did not increase rates of maternal death or cardiac or respiratory arrest when compared with placebo. [55]

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Eclampsia
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium sulfate monitoring Therapeutic levels : Elevated levels of magnesium sulfate can cause adverse events, including respiratory depression and cardiac arrest. Therefore, clinicians should monitor for clinical signs of toxicity, including decreased urine output, absent deep tendon reflexes, and respiratory depression. Clinicians should assess magnesium serum levels every 4 to 6 hours in patients with renal impairment due to the increased risk of toxicity. [2] [11] Toxicity management : In patients demonstrating signs of magnesium toxicity (eg, respiratory depression) or the serum level is >9.6 mg/dL, the magnesium sulfate infusion should be discontinued, and calcium gluconate 10% solution (10 mL IV) can be administered over 3 min as an antidote. [11] Magnesium sulfate duration : Continuing magnesium sulfate administration is recommended for 24 hours after delivery or the last seizure. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Deep Tendon Reflexes -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Deep tendon reflexes are a powerful tool to evaluate a pregnant patient's response to magnesium infusions. Magnesium sulfate is the primary medication used to prevent and manage eclamptic seizures, as it exerts its effects by depressing the central nervous system (CNS). One of the first signs of magnesium toxicity is new-onset loss of DTRs, which is a hard sign that magnesium infusion should be immediately stopped. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Magnesium -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the physiological processes in which magnesium is involved. Assess the indications for endogenously administered magnesium therapy. Evaluate the contraindications and toxicity of magnesium therapy. Communicate interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination to properly use magnesium to improve patient outcomes in the varied scenarios where magnesium is effective. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Magnesium Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium toxicity is commonly caused by the overuse of magnesium-containing medication or the under-excretion of magnesium by the kidneys. Early recognition, as well as a high index of suspicion, is critical when diagnosing magnesium toxicity before the onset of fatal complications such as hypotension, respiratory paralysis, and cardiac arrest. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of magnesium toxicity and highlights the interprofessional healthcare team's role in evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Tranexamic Acid -- Toxicity -- Management of Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: Oral overdose is usually managed with supportive care and does not require hospitalization in the absence of co-ingestants or symptoms. Intrathecal exposure requires urgent seizure control with benzodiazepines and barbiturates. Additional treatments may include magnesium, mannitol, dexamethasone, propofol, or inhaled anesthetics. CSF exchange has been reported to be helpful in some cases. Clear labeling, adherence to proper pharmacy practices, and implementation of operating room safety protocols are strongly recommended to prevent such events. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Magnesium Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the risk factors for developing magnesium toxicity. Apply the typical presentation of a patient with magnesium toxicity. Determine the management options available for magnesium toxicity. Communicate the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care for patients with magnesium toxicity. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypermagnesemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Particular clinical conditions require a specific approach. For instance, during the management of eclampsia, the magnesium infusion is stopped if urine output drops to less than 80 mL (in 4 hours), deep tendon reflexes are absent, or the respiratory rate is below 12 breaths/minute. A 10% calcium gluconate or chloride solution (10 mL intravenously repeatable over 5 minutes) can serve as an antidote.

retrieved_docs.title: Magnesium Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Obtaining a thorough history is paramount when diagnosing magnesium toxicity, as magnesium levels are not checked in a routine workup, and the symptoms that patients can present with tend to be nonspecific. As previously discussed, magnesium toxicity results from under-excretion, over-consumption, and storage displacement. The history should be focused on their etiology, such as a history of chronic kidney disease, the use of magnesium-containing medications such as antacids and laxatives, and recent chemotherapy treatment. Patients on dialysis who may have missed their treatments are also especially at risk. A physical exam is difficult as patients may present with nonspecific findings such as visual changes, flushing, muscle paralysis, and somnolence. However, loss of patellar reflexes should raise suspicion for magnesium toxicity. Patients receiving a magnesium infusion, such as those with preeclampsia, require a continuous reassessment of patellar reflexes. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Diphenhydramine -- Toxicity -- Management of Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium sulfate: should be administered intravenously for a prolonged QT interval

retrieved_docs.title: Nerve Agents -- Treatment / Management -- Decontamination and Supportive Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Excessive AChE stimulation can cause bradycardia, and QT interval prolongation can lead to ventricular dysrhythmias, particularly torsades de points. If QT interval prolongation is seen, intravenous (IV) magnesium should be administered. [24] [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Magnesium -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: For contraindications, factors to consider are renal function, pregnancy, and neuromuscular disease. It is crucial to assess renal function before giving magnesium. Renal failure can cause decreased magnesium excretion, which can lead to toxicity. Closely monitor magnesium levels in patients with reduced renal function. [2] Patients with neuromuscular disease, such as myasthenia gravis, must be closely monitored to see if they are being given magnesium. Magnesium inhibits the release of acetylcholine, which can cause deterioration. Magnesium is considered class D for pregnancy. Skeletal demineralization, hypocalcemia, and hypermagnesemia are seen with long-term use, more than 5 to 7 days, of magnesium, which is necessary for pre-term labor treatment. There are no contraindications for magnesium supplements with other prescriptions. However, various drugs increase or decrease the concentration of either magnesium or the prescribed medication. Magnesium may be used in pregnancy and lactation; there is no expected risk of fetal or infant harm based on limited data in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Glycoside and Digoxin Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- Supportive Care and Antiarrhythmic Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Supraventricular tachycardia tends to be managed with short-acting beta-blockers. Phenytoin has been shown to suppress digoxin-induced tachyarrhythmias as well. Another option is lidocaine when managing ventricular arrhythmias. Atropine may be used to manage bradycardia. The use of magnesium is not recommended as it can worsen bradycardia and AV blocks. Cardioversion is not recommended as it can precipitate ventricular arrhythmias; instead, defibrillation may be used according to ACLS protocol.

retrieved_docs.title: Arsenic Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Dimercaptopropanesulfonic acid (DMPS): 3 to 5 milligrams/kg given IM every 4 hours administered as 5% solution for 2 days followed by succimer or 3 to 5mg/kg IM every 6 to 12 hours (DMPS does not have approval in the USA). Succimer(2,3-Dimercaptosuccinic acid): 10 milligrams/kg PO every 8 hours for 5 days followed by 10milligrams/kg PO q12 hours. The endpoint for chelation therapy is 24-hour urinary arsenic of less than 50 ug/L. In the case of ventricular tachycardia or torsades de point, treat as necessary with magnesium sulfate, lidocaine, amiodarone, and defibrillation. It is critical to avoid the use of IA (procainamide, quinidine, disopyramide), IC and III antiarrhythmics secondary to risk QT prolongation. Potassium, calcium, magnesium levels should be monitored and maintained within normal limits to avoid worsening of QT prolongation. Treat seizures with benzodiazepine and general anesthesia as necessary. Arsenic exposure to the skin is readily removed with soap and water.

retrieved_docs.title: Magnesium Toxicity -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional team that provides a holistic and integrated approach to care can help achieve the best possible outcomes. If magnesium toxicity occurs, prompt treatment cannot be undermined. The patient must be stabilized as early as possible and subsequently monitored closely. Major complications associated with magnesium toxicity can be avoided if the interprofessional team can promptly diagnose and treat this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Anxiolytics and Sedative-Hypnotics Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: SSRI/SNRI treatment involves monitoring for signs and symptoms of serotonin syndrome, seizures, cardiac conduction abnormalities, and QT interval prolongation. BZDs can be given to those with signs of serotonin syndrome or seizures. Sodium bicarbonate is an option for those with cardiac toxicity. Magnesium sulfate intravenously can be given to those at risk of developing torsades de pointes. Additionally, immediate non-synchronized electric defibrillation is indicated for patients with hemodynamic instability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2795
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Nephrology Terminology includes the concept of قصور الكلى (renal failure). Within this clinical framework, magnesium gluconate is contraindicated in patients with قصور الكلى, indicating that the supplement should be avoided in individuals who have renal failure. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for clinicians referencing Pediatric Nephrology Terminology to ensure safe medication management for patients with kidney dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1752
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium aspartate (aspartát hořečnatý) is contraindicated in patients with renal failure (قصور الكلى). Because impaired kidney function reduces the ability to excrete excess magnesium, administration of magnesium aspartate in this population can lead to accumulation of magnesium and potentially cause hypermagnesemia, which may result in neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and respiratory complications. Therefore, clinicians should avoid prescribing magnesium aspartate to individuals with renal failure and consider alternative strategies for managing magnesium deficiency in this group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 373
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium citrate is an osmotic laxative that may be used to treat إِمْساك, but its administration is contraindicated in patients with ثقب معوي because it can worsen a perforated intestine. إِمْساك is also clinically associated with تَقَيُّء, indicating that constipation and diarrhea often occur together in functional bowel disorders. When considering magnesium citrate for symptom relief, clinicians must ensure the absence of ثقب معوي and be aware of the potential shift from إِمْساك to تَقَيُّء, tailoring treatment to maintain safe and effective gastrointestinal management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1458
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب السبيل البولي (urinary tract disorder) is recognized as having a broader relationship with قصور الكلى (renal failure), indicating that disturbances of the urinary system are often linked to or may progress to impaired kidney function. In this clinical context, magnesium gluconate is contraindicated in patients with قصور الكلى, meaning that the use of this magnesium supplement should be avoided in individuals who have renal failure to prevent potential adverse effects. Understanding the connection between urinary tract disorders and renal failure, as well as the medication contraindication, is essential for safe patient management and appropriate therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 197
retrieved_docs.content: are contraindicated in patients with renal failure, reflecting the need to avoid electrolyte accumulation in compromised kidneys. This safety restriction extends to other magnesium salts—magnesium sulfate, magnesium phosphate, magnesium citrate, magnesium oxide, magnesium aspartate, magnesium gluconate and magnesium pidolate—as well as to calcium‑magnesium carbonate and calcium carbonate when used as therapeutic agents. Magnesium sulfate serves as the parent compound for calcium‑ and magnesium‑containing products, is an ingredient of cardioplegic solutions (Del Nido) and irrigation solutions (containing magnesium sulfate, monobasic potassium phosphate, potassium chloride, sodium chloride and sodium phosphate dihydrate), and belongs to the class of osmotically acting laxatives together with magnesium citrate. Magnesium citrate, magnesium oxide, magnesium aspartate, magnesium gluconate and magnesium pidolate share contraindications in renal failure; magnesium citrate is additionally

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 197

Collectively, the community’s knowledge interconnects magnesium‑based supplements, electrolyte management, ophthalmic irrigation, cardiac cardioplegia, oral, topical and injectable formulations, dental care, and functional gastrointestinal disorders through shared ingredients (magnesium chloride, potassium chloride, calcium carbonate, sodium monofluorophosphate) and common safety considerations, particularly the avoidance of these salts in individuals with impaired renal function.

retrieved_docs.content: for specific counter‑measures to manage toxic exposures. The inclusion of protective substances among potentially poisonous agents highlights that any chemical, regardless of intended safety purpose, may become harmful at inappropriate doses or under certain physiological conditions. Recognizing these classifications supports clinicians, toxicologists and public‑health professionals in selecting appropriate drugs, monitoring therapy, promptly identifying toxic exposures, implementing decontamination and treatment strategies, and educating patients about risks associated with both prescription medications and seemingly benign chemicals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 367
retrieved_docs.content: زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية (ارتفاع الفجوة الأنيونية) is clinically associated with قصور الكلى (الفشل الكلوي). In patients with قصور الكلى, the use of aspartát hořečnatý (مغنيسيوم أسبارت) is contraindicated because impaired renal function can lead to accumulation and toxicity of magnesium. Consequently, the presence of an elevated anion gap may signal underlying renal failure, and clinicians should avoid prescribing magnesium aspartate to individuals diagnosed with قصور الكلى.
